title: Testicular Torsion
chief_complaint: My left testicle hurts really bad. It started suddenly while I was sitting in class. I feel nauseous and I think I'm going to vomit. It's never happened before.

physical_exam.Genitalia: Left testicle: Swollen, tender, high-riding (retracted upward), horizontal lie. Absent cremasteric reflex on left (diagnostic). Right testicle: Normal position, vertical lie. Cremasteric reflex present on right.
physical_exam.Abdomen: Mild lower abdominal tenderness. No guarding. No rebound.
physical_exam.Urinary: No dysuria, no frequency, no hematuria (differentiates from epididymitis).

hidden_data.doppler_us: Left testicle: Absent flow on color Doppler. Normal echotexture (no mass - differentiates from tumor). Right testicle: Normal flow.
hidden_data.urinalysis: Normal. No WBCs, no nitrites (rules out epididymitis/orchitis).

key_learning_points: Testicular torsion = twisting of spermatic cord → occlusion of testicular blood flow → ischemia → infarction. Time-dependent: Salvage rate >90% if <6h, 50% at 12h, <10% at >24h. IRREVERSIBLE ischemia after 6-8 hours

Bell-clapper deformity = testis lacks normal posterior fixation to tunica vaginalis → free to rotate within scrotum. Bilateral in 80% (always fix contralateral testis). Peak age: Neonatal (extravaginal) and 12-18 years (intravaginal)

Classic presentation: Sudden onset severe unilateral testicular pain + Nausea/vomiting + High-riding testis with horizontal lie + Absent cremasteric reflex. Acute scrotum in adolescent = torsion until proven otherwise

Diagnosis: CLINICAL. Do NOT delay surgery for imaging. Doppler US if equivocal: Absent flow confirms torsion, flow present does NOT rule out (intermittent torsion, partial torsion). Surgery: Scrotal exploration. Detorse testis (medial rotation). If viable (improved color, bleeding after tunica incision): Orchiopexy (fix with 3-point suture). If non-viable (no improvement): Orchiectomy. ALWAYS perform contralateral orchiopexy (bell-clapper deformity bilateral)

Manual detorsion: Temporizing measure if OR not immediately available. 'Open book' maneuver: Rotate testis laterally (like opening a book). Success if pain decreases and testis descends. Still requires surgical exploration and fixation
Medicolegal emergency: One of the most common causes of malpractice lawsuits in emergency medicine and urology. Missed torsion = loss of testicle. LOW threshold for scrotal exploration
